questions.prompt: In Chapter 1, the discussion about robots primarily functions as what kind of opening move by the author?
options: A metaphor for Sophie's dislike of Joanna
A prediction that the plot will become science-fiction driven
A warning that technology will replace education
A wedge that turns ordinary talk into a question about what a human being is

questions.prompt: Sophie's house 'at the end of the world' (edge of suburb, woods beyond) most strongly signals:
options: A psychological boundary; she is positioned at the edge of the familiar, facing the unknown
A literal survival theme; nature will be the central conflict
A future setting shift; the story will move into the forest permanently
A socioeconomic clue; she is poorer than her classmates

questions.prompt: Joanna's 'brain as an advanced computer' claim is best read as representing which philosophical stance?
options: Materialism / reductionism (mind explained fully by physical processes)
Idealism (reality is fundamentally mental)
Skepticism (nothing can be known)
Dualism (mind and body are separate substances)

questions.prompt: Sophie's hesitation about the computer analogy implies a commitment (even if unformed) to:
options: The belief that humans have no physical basis at all
The view that robots are morally evil
The idea that lived experience (subjectivity) isn't captured by 'hardware' descriptions
The belief that school should teach only practical skills

questions.prompt: The chapter title 'The Garden of Eden' is most likely doing what, at this early stage?
options: Indicating a romantic subplot is about to start
Hinting Sophie will study theology instead of philosophy
Referring to a real park Sophie will visit
Framing the story as an origin-question narrative: beginnings, innocence, and first questions

questions.prompt: How does Democritus's atom theory challenge the concept of an eternal, immortal soul?
options: It proves that the soul reincarnates into new physical forms after death.
It suggests the soul is made of material 'soul atoms' that disperse in all directions when the brain disintegrates.
It argues that only the gods, not humans, possess immortal souls.
It claims that the soul is an illusion created by the fear of death.

questions.prompt: What significant shift in human thinking is represented by the transition from consulting the Oracle at Delphi to the medical practices of Hippocrates?
options: A shift from democratic decision-making to relying on expert priests.
A shift from polytheism to strict monotheism.
A shift from seeking supernatural explanations for health and history to seeking natural ones.
A shift from treating physical ailments to prioritizing psychological therapy.

questions.prompt: Why does Socrates compare his philosophical method to the work of a midwife?
options: Because he believed that new knowledge must be painfully extracted from nature.
Because he helped 'deliver' truths that individuals already possessed within their innate reason.
Because he believed that philosophy was a nurturing, feminine pursuit.
Because he guided young people through the painful transition into adulthood.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what distinguishes a true philosopher from a 'sophist' in the context of ancient Athens?
options: A philosopher recognizes their own ignorance and strives for insight, while a sophist is a self-opinionated know-it-all who teaches for money.
A philosopher relies on written texts, while a sophist relies purely on oral debate.
A philosopher believes in the Greek gods, while a sophist is strictly an atheist.
A philosopher charges money for teaching, while a sophist teaches for free.

questions.prompt: In Plato's philosophy, what does the 'gingerbread mold' metaphor illustrate about the physical world?
options: That the universe was created mechanically without any divine purpose.
That human beings impose their own subjective patterns onto meaningless matter.
That all sensory objects are temporary, imperfect copies of eternal, immutable forms or 'ideas.'
That physical objects are perfect reflections of the creator's mind.

questions.prompt: What psychological or societal reality does Plato's 'Myth of the Cave' attempt to expose?
options: The human tendency to mistake fleeting sensory illusions (shadows) for ultimate reality, and the danger faced by those who try to reveal the truth.
The necessity of religious rituals to overcome the darkness of human nature.
The evolutionary origins of humanity as cave-dwelling creatures.
The danger of isolating oneself from the political life of the city-state.

questions.prompt: How does Aristotle's view of 'ideas' or 'forms' directly contradict his teacher Plato's view?
options: Aristotle claimed that forms are constantly changing, while Plato claimed they are eternal.
Aristotle believed forms exist only in the human imagination, while Plato believed they exist in nature.
Aristotle believed only living creatures have forms, while Plato applied forms to inanimate objects.
Aristotle argued that 'forms' are the specific characteristics inherent within physical things, not separate entities in a higher realm.

questions.prompt: When explaining why it rains, Aristotle includes the 'final cause.' What does this reveal about his worldview?
options: He viewed nature as purely mechanical and devoid of any underlying meaning.
He thought that human beings have the power to alter the weather through ethical behavior.
He believed that natural phenomena have an inherent purpose, such as raining so that plants and animals can grow.
He believed that all natural processes are driven by a conscious, divine puppeteer.

questions.prompt: How did Diogenes the Cynic demonstrate his philosophical belief when visited by Alexander the Great?
options: He begged for food and shelter to show that humanity relies on mutual compassion.
He demanded a position of power to enact social reform.
He engaged Alexander in a dialectical debate to prove the futility of war.
He asked the emperor to step aside because he was blocking the sun, proving that true happiness doesn't depend on material or political power.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what is a key difference between the traditional Indo-European and Semitic views of history?
options: Indo-Europeans have a cyclic view of history, while Semites have a linear view of history.
Indo-Europeans believe history is controlled by one God, while Semites believe it is guided by many gods.
Indo-Europeans record history primarily through writing, while Semites rely solely on oral traditions.
Indo-Europeans view history as an illusion, while Semites view it as a strict scientific progression.
